segments.paradiso-13-g78: 'Non ho parlato sì' is a negative perfect: 'Non' negates the auxiliary 'ho' + past participle 'parlato' (I have spoken / I spoke). The adverb 'sì' here means 'in such a way' (manner), so the phrase = 'I did not speak in such a way'.
segments.paradiso-13-g79: This is a result clause introduced by 'che': the whole '…sì, che tu non posse ben veder…' = 'not so that you could not well see…'. 'posse' is an archaic/poetic form of 'potere' (can/possible), used here in the sense of ability (present/subjunctive-register in older Italian). 'ben veder' = 'to see clearly'. 'ch'el fu re' is a subordinate clause ('that he was king') with 'fu' = passato (he was).
segments.paradiso-13-g80: 'che chiese senno' continues the identification of the person: 'who asked (chiese, passato remoto) for wisdom (senno)'. 'acciò che' = 'so that / in order that' introduces a purpose/result clause and takes the subjunctive 'fosse' (imperfect subjunctive) to indicate purpose in the past: 'so that he might be a sufficient king.'
segments.paradiso-13-g81: 'non per sapere' = 'not in order to know' (negative purpose). The relative clause 'in che enno li motor di qua sù' = 'in which are the movers from here upward', where 'enno' is an archaic/poetic form of 'sono' (they are). 'li motor' = 'the movers' (agents that move, i.e. celestial movers).
segments.paradiso-13-g82: This begins an alternative clause 'o se…' = 'or whether…'. The sequence 'necesse con contingente mai necesse fenno' uses older wording: read as 'whether necessity (necesse), together with contingency, ever made (fenno, past plural 'fecero') [things] necessary'. 'fenno' is a past-tense form meaning 'they made/did'.
segments.paradiso-13-g83: 'non si est dare primum motum esse' is impersonal/abstract: 'non si est' = 'it is not (proper/possible to say)'. The infinitival sequence 'dare primum motum esse' expresses the idea 'to ascribe/give the first motion to being' (a philosophical claim about the origin of motion). 'si' here is the impersonal 'one/it'.
segments.paradiso-13-g84: Another alternative introduced by 'o se' = 'or whether'. 'del mezzo cerchio far si puote' = 'is it possible to make from a semicircle' where 'si puote' is an archaic impersonal form (modern 'si può'). The result-clause 'trïangol sì ch’un retto non avesse' uses 'sì ch’…avesse' = 'so that it would not have' with 'avesse' in the subjunctive (archaic sequence after 'sì che'), meaning 'a triangle such that it would not have a right angle.'
segments.paradiso-13-g85: Onde is a conjunctive adverb here meaning ‘therefore’ or ‘whence,’ used to introduce a conclusion or consequence.
segments.paradiso-13-g86: Se is the conditional conjunction ‘if’. Ciò = ‘that/what’ and ch’ = che (relative ‘that/which’). Io is an emphatic subject pronoun ‘I,’ and dissi is passato remoto ‘I said.’ E questo note: note is an archaic second‑person imperative/subjunctive form meaning ‘note/pay attention to’; here the whole phrase means ‘if you note what I said and this.’
segments.paradiso-13-g87: Regal prudenza è — predicate first for emphasis: ‘royal prudence is…’. Quel vedere impari: quel = ‘that’, vedere is used as an abstract noun ‘seeing/sight’ or ‘vision’ (a way of perceiving/judging), and impari (here archaic ‘impar’ = unequal/uneven) qualifies it: ‘that unequal sight.’ In che introduces the relative clause ‘in which’ and lo stral di mia intenzion percuote is a metaphorical construction: lo stral (‘the shaft/arrow/ray’) of my intention (di mia intenzion) percuote (present indicative ‘strikes’ or ‘impacts’).
segments.paradiso-13-g88: e se = ‘and if’. Al is the contraction a + il ‘to the’. “Surse” (surso) here is a poetic noun ‘the risen one(s)’; drizzi is second‑person present (you direct/you turn) or subjunctive/imperative form ‘you turn’ (addressing the reader), li occhi chiari = ‘your clear/bright eyes’ (li as article before occhi; chiari adjective).
segments.paradiso-13-g89: Vedrai is future second person singular ‘you will see.’ Aver here is an infinitive used in an object‑infinitive construction (‘you will see [that there is / that one has]’); solamente = ‘only/merely.’ Respectto (respetto) followed by ai regi means ‘respect toward kings’ (respetto = ‘respect’). The clause is idiomatic: ‘you will see that only respect is accorded to kings.’
segments.paradiso-13-g90: Che introduces a relative clause ‘who/which’. Son is the contracted son(o) = sono, present indicative ‘are’. Molti = ‘many’ (predicate adjective agreeing with the implied kings).
segments.paradiso-13-g91: E = ‘and’. ’ buon is a substantive use of the adjective buono (the apostrophe marks the elided plural ending: ‘good ones’) and son rari = ‘are rare’; the phrase means ‘and good ones are rare.’ The word order is inverted for emphasis (adjective substantivized as subject).
segments.paradiso-13-g92: Con questa distinzion = 'With this distinction.' 'Con' is the preposition 'with' introducing the means; 'distinzion' is an archaic/poetic spelling of 'distinzione'. 'Prendi' is the second-person singular imperative of prendere ('take'); the object is the elided article + noun '’l mio detto' (’l = il), i.e. 'my saying.'
segments.paradiso-13-g93: e così puote star con quel che credi del primo padre e del nostro Diletto = 'and thus it can stand with what you believe concerning the first Father and our Beloved.' 'Puote' is an archaic form corresponding to modern 'può' (third-person singular present of potere, 'can'); it governs the infinitive 'star' (stare, 'to stand/be valid'). 'Quel che' = 'that which/what' (relative clause), and 'del' is the contraction of 'di + il.' 'Diletto' here is a substantive, capitalized, meaning 'Beloved.'
segments.paradiso-13-g94: E questo ti sia sempre piombo a’ piedi = 'And let this be always lead at your feet.' 'Sia' is the present subjunctive of essere used optatively (a wish or admonition: 'may this be'). 'Piombo a’ piedi' is a figurative expression ('lead at the feet') meaning a burden that slows movement; 'a’ piedi' is a poetic form of 'ai piedi' ('at the feet').
segments.paradiso-13-g95: per farti mover lento com’ uom lasso e al sì e al no che tu non vedi: = 'to make you move slowly like a weary man, and as to the yes and the no which you do not see.' 'Per' here means 'in order to'; 'farti' is the infinitive 'fare' with the clitic 'ti' attached (literally 'to make you'); it governs the infinitive 'mover.' 'com’' is contraction of 'come' meaning 'as/like.' 'Al sì e al no' uses the article with 'sì'/'no' to mean 'with respect to the yes and the no.' 'Che tu non vedi' is a relative clause with explicit subject pronoun and present indicative 'non vedi' ('you do not see/perceive').
segments.paradiso-13-g96: ché quelli è tra li stolti bene a basso, = 'for he is among the fools of low estate,' 'ché' = 'because/for.' 'Quelli' here is a demonstrative used as a personal pronoun ('that one' = 'he'); 'è' is the present indicative 'is.' The phrase 'bene a basso' is idiomatic/archaic: 'bene' meaning 'goods/status/estate' and 'a basso' 'of low,' so 'of low estate.'
segments.paradiso-13-g97: che sanza distinzione afferma e nega ne l’un così come ne l’altro passo; = 'who, without distinction, affirms and denies in the one as in the other case.' 'Che' is the relative pronoun 'who/that.' 'Sanza' = 'senza' (without) used in the archaic spelling 'sanza'; 'afferma e nega' are present-indicative characteristics ('he affirms and denies'). The phrase 'ne l’un... ne l’altro' means 'in the one just as in the other' (ne = partitive/locative element used in older Italian), so the clause criticizes inconsistency.
segments.paradiso-13-g98: perch’ elli ’ncontra che più volte piega l’oppinïon corrente in falsa parte, = 'because he, on the contrary, more often bends prevailing opinion to the false side.' 'Perch’' = 'perché' ('because'); '’ncontra' (incontra) here functions adverbially/idiomatically as 'on the contrary' or 'contrariwise.' 'Che' introduces the relative clause 'who more often bends' and 'piega' is present indicative 'bends/turns.' 'L’oppinïon corrente' = 'the current/prevailing opinion,' and 'in falsa parte' = 'to the false side.'
segments.paradiso-13-g99: e poi l’affetto l’intelletto lega. = 'and then passion binds the intellect.' 'Poi' = 'then/afterwards.' Word order is Subject (l’affetto) + Verb (lega) + Object (l’intelletto); 'lega' is present indicative 'binds' and expresses causal sequence: once opinion is bent, affection (passion) restrains or binds the understanding.
segments.paradiso-13-g53: La cera di costoro e chi la duce non sta d’un modo; is a clause saying two things are not the same. La cera di costoro = 'the wax of these people' (La + noun + prepositional phrase). e chi la duce links a the preceding noun with an appended relative noun phrase 'and he who leads/molds it' (chi = 'who'); duce is a noun 'leader/one who guides or shapes'. non sta d’un modo uses the present indicative stare (sta) with the fixed expression stare d’un modo = 'be of one manner/kind' (negated by non), so literally 'is not of one manner'.
segments.paradiso-13-g54: e però sotto ’l segno idëale poi più e men traluce. e però = 'and therefore'; sotto ’l segno idëale = 'under the ideal sign' (sotto + definite article + noun + adjective). poi più e men is an adverbial cluster 'then/moreover, more and less' qualifying traluce. traluce is a present indicative (from tralucere/tralurre) meaning 'shine through / be revealed', so the clause says the ideal sign makes it show through to a greater or lesser degree.
segments.paradiso-13-g55: Ond’ elli avvien ch’un medesimo legno, begins with Ond' (a poetic form of 'onde'/'whence' or 'therefore'), followed by elli which here functions as the indirect object 'to them' (it befalls them); avvien is the present impersonal/3rd-person singular 'happens' (avvenire), ch’ = che 'that', and un medesimo legno = 'one the same piece of wood'. The clause sets up the example introduced by 'whence/therefore it happens that...'.
segments.paradiso-13-g56: secondo specie, is an adverbial phrase 'according to species/kind' where secondo = 'according to' and specie = 'kind, sort'; it modifies how the single 'legno' is considered (i.e. in different species/forms).
segments.paradiso-13-g57: meglio e peggio frutta; contains meglio 'better' and peggio 'worse' joined by e, with frutta (third-person singular of fruttare or noun 'fruit') meaning 'yields/produces fruit' — here used as 'bears better and worse fruit' (i.e. gives better or worse results).
segments.paradiso-13-g58: e voi nascete con diverso ingegno. e = 'and', voi = 'you' (plural), nascete is present indicative 'are born' (nascere) used here to state human difference, con diverso ingegno = 'with different intellect/temperament' where ingegno means natural disposition or talent. The clause contrasts human variety with the earlier example.
segments.paradiso-13-g59: Se fosse a punto la cera dedutta e fosse il cielo in sua virtù supprema, is a double protasis using the imperfect subjunctive fosse ('were') to set up a hypothetical: 'If the wax were to the point/fully prepared' (a punto) and 'if the sky/heaven were in its supreme virtue'. la cera dedutta = 'the wax brought/derived' (here 'worked/refined'); il cielo in sua virtù supprema = 'the heaven in its highest power'. The subjunctive marks counterfactual/hypothetical condition.
segments.paradiso-13-g60: la luce del suggel parrebbe tutta; has the conditional parrebbe ('would seem') with subject la luce del suggel = 'the light of the seal/imprint'. tutta qualifies parrebbe: 'would seem entirely/complete.' This is the apodosis (result clause) of the putative condition in the previous segment.
segments.paradiso-13-g61: ma la natura la dà sempre scema, contrasts with ma = 'but'. la natura = 'nature' (subject). la (here a direct object pronoun) refers back to 'la luce' and dà = present 'gives'; sempre = 'always'; scema is adjectival/verb form meaning 'diminished, scant' (i.e. 'gives it always reduced'). The clause says that actual nature reduces the perfection described above.
segments.paradiso-13-g62: similemente operando a l’artista ch’a l’abito de l’arte ha man che trema. similemente = 'similarly'; operando is a present participle 'operating/working' and takes the agent phrase 'a l’artista' = 'upon the artist'. ch’ = 'who' introduces a relative clause: a l’abito de l’arte ha man che trema = 'who, in the practice/habit of the art, has a hand that trembles' (ha mano che trema). The image compares nature's imperfecting action to an artist whose trembling hand spoils the work.
segments.paradiso-13-g63: Però se ’l caldo amor la chiara vista de la prima virtù dispone e segna, is a conditional clause: Però = 'therefore'; se = 'if' (introducing the condition); ’l caldo amor = 'the warm love' (caldo amor = passionate or fervent love); la chiara vista = 'the clear sight/vision'; de la prima virtù = 'of the first/primary virtue' (i.e. the highest power); dispone e segna are present indicatives 'disposes/ inclines and marks', meaning love inclines and stamps that sight. The clause sets the positive condition for full perfection.
segments.paradiso-13-g64: tutta la perfezion quivi s’acquista. tutta la perfezion = 'all perfection' (tutta qualifies perfezion); quivi = 'there' (adverb pointing to that place/condition); s’acquista is a reflexive/passive-like present form of acquistare 'is acquired/gained' (s' + acquista), so 'all perfection is acquired there.' This is the result clause (apodosis) of the conditional.
segments.paradiso-13-g65: Così is an adverb meaning 'thus' placed at the clause start for emphasis. Fu fatta is a past passive formed with fu (past tense of essere as auxiliary) + fatta (past participle) meaning 'was made'; già ('already') modifies the action. The subject la terra follows the verb (inverted order typical of poetry), and degna di tutta l’animal perfezïone is a predicate adjective with a 'di' complement meaning 'worthy of the whole perfection of living creatures.'
segments.paradiso-13-g66: Repeating Così fu fatta uses the same passive construction 'was made'; here la Vergine is the subject and pregna is an adjective 'pregnant' placed after the noun (postpositive adjective), yielding 'the Virgin was made pregnant.' The clause echoes the previous structure for contrast/parallelism.
segments.paradiso-13-g67: Sì ch’ (sì che) introduces a result/consequence clause 'so that/therefore'; ch’ is the shortened conjunction 'che' ('that'). Io commendo is first-person singular present ('I commend' / 'I entrust' / 'I approve of'); commendo here has the sense 'commend/entrust' which is not given in the provided sense list. Tua oppinïone is the direct object 'your opinion.'
segments.paradiso-13-g68: Che introduces the content clause 'that...'. L’umana natura is the subject 'human nature.' The archaic negative sequence mai non fue né fia uses fue (past 'was') and fia (future 'will be') to mean 'never was nor will be'; mai here functions as 'never' in the negative construction. Qual fu in quelle due persone = 'as it was in those two persons,' with qual = 'as' and fu = past 'was.'
segments.paradiso-13-g69: Or = 'now' as a discourse marker. S’i’ here stands for se io ('if I'); the contraction s’ is an archaic/clitic form (not listed in the provided senses). Procedesse is the imperfect subjunctive of procedere used in a conditional protasis ('if I did/proceeded not'), and avanti piùe ('any further') is an adverbial phrase; the negative non negates procedesse.
segments.paradiso-13-g70: Dunque is an interjection/conjunction ('therefore' / 'then') used here to begin the quoted question that follows.
segments.paradiso-13-g71: Come introduces the interrogative 'how'; costui is the demonstrative subject 'this man/this one'; fu is simple past 'was'; sanza (archaic sansa) means 'without'; pare here means 'peer/equal' (from Latin par) rather than the verb 'to seem' — an archaic nominal use not present in the provided sense list.
segments.paradiso-13-g72: Comincerebber is an archaic 3rd-person-plural conditional (modern comincerebbero) meaning 'they would begin.' The verb precedes the subject here (inversion) and le parole tue ('your words') is the subject phrase; the overall sense is 'your words would begin.'
segments.paradiso-13-g73: Ma 'but' opens the sentence. Perché here functions as a purpose connector 'so that,' and paia is the present subjunctive of paiare ('may appear' / 'seem'), expressing purpose/result; ben strengthens the clause ('clearly'/'well'). Ciò che non pare = 'that which does not appear.'
segments.paradiso-13-g74: Pensa is an imperative or exhortative present 'think / consider' addressed to the reader; chi era is an indirect question 'who he was' with era as the imperfect/indicative past 'was.'
segments.paradiso-13-g75: E = 'and.' La cagion is 'the cause/reason.' Che introduces a relative clause 'that moved him'; ’l is the clitic lo ('him' / 'it') attached before mosse, and mosse is the past tense (passato remoto) of muovere 'moved.' The provided senses for the clitic and mosse do not match the verbal/clitic readings, so those get -1.
segments.paradiso-13-g76: Quando = 'when.' Fu detto is a passive past ('was said') with fu as auxiliary; Detto is the past participle 'said.' The quoted Chiedi is an imperative 'Ask!' (the exact words spoken).
segments.paradiso-13-g77: A + infinitive (a dimandare) expresses purpose/result: 'to ask' (dimandare = 'to ask/request'); it completes the idea 'moved him to ask.'
segments.paradiso-13-g1: A vocative/imperative address: Dante calls on the reader to 'Imagine'. In the poem this form functions as an exhortation to form a mental picture; it is archaic/poetic in form and stands alone as a sentence-initial interjection.
segments.paradiso-13-g2: 'chi' = 'whoever' (indefinite relative) + verblike clause. 'bene intender' is an infinitive construction meaning 'to understand well'. 'cupe' is an archaic form (not an adjective here) expressing desire/wish (roughly 'may wish' / 'wants to'); the whole clause = 'whoever wishes to understand well what I have now seen'. 'ritegna l’image' is an independent imperative/indicative instruction: 'and retain/hold the image'.
segments.paradiso-13-g3: A simple temporal subordinate: 'mentre' = 'while', 'ch’io dico' = 'that I say' (present indicative). It instructs the reader to keep the mental image during Dante's speech.
segments.paradiso-13-g4: 'come' = 'like/as' and the noun phrase 'ferma rupe' = 'a fixed/firm cliff'. 'ferma' here is adjectival/poetic (firm, immovable); the dash marks a vivid simile that interrupts the line.
segments.paradiso-13-g5: Main descriptive clause: 'quindici stelle' is the subject; 'che ’n diverse plage lo ciel avvivan' = relative clause 'which in different regions enliven the sky'. 'di tanto sereno che soperchia de l’aere ogne compage' is a result clause introduced by 'che' ('so calm that...'), where 'soperchia' is a verb meaning 'overpowers/exceeds' and 'compage' = 'structure/framework' of the air.
segments.paradiso-13-g6: 'imagini' here repeats the exhortation 'imagine' (poetic imperative/indicative of 'imaginare'); it introduces the next image. 'quel carro a cu’ il seno basta del nostro cielo e notte e giorno' — relative/linked clause where 'a cu’' = 'to which' (archaic 'al quale'), 'il seno basta...' = 'its breast suffices for our heaven both night and day' (i.e. the chariot's bosom contains/sustains our heaven continuously).
segments.paradiso-13-g7: 'sì ch’' introduces a result: 'so that'. 'al volger del temo' = 'at the turning/passing of time' (archaic spelling 'temo' for 'tempo'); 'non vien meno' is an idiom meaning 'does not fail / is not diminished'. The whole = 'so that when time turns, it does not fail.'
segments.paradiso-13-g8: Another 'imagini' ('imagine') clause introducing 'la bocca di quel corno' = 'the mouth/opening of that horn'. 'che si comincia' uses the reflexive 'si' with intransitive 'cominciare' to mean 'which begins'. 'in punta de lo stelo' = 'at the point of the shaft/stalk', and 'a cui la prima rota va dintorno' = 'around which the first wheel goes' (relative 'a cui' = 'around which').
segments.paradiso-13-g9: 'aver fatto di sé due segni in cielo' is a perfect-infinitive construction: 'to have made (or having made) two signs of itself in the sky'. 'di sé' = 'of itself' (reflexive emphasis). This phrase describes the horn's having produced two celestial marks.
segments.paradiso-13-g10: 'qual fece la figliuola di Minoi' = 'such as the daughter of Minos made' — 'qual' introduces a comparison; 'fece' is simple past 'made/did'. 'allora che sentì di morte il gelo' = temporal clause 'when she felt the chill of death' — 'sentì' = 'felt' here.
segments.paradiso-13-g11: A brief descriptive clause: 'e l’un ne l’altro aver li raggi suoi' = 'and one had its rays in the other' (word-for-word: 'and the one in the other to have its rays'). Word order is inverted (poetic) but the sense is reciprocal possession of rays.
segments.paradiso-13-g12: 'e amendue girarsi per maniera' = 'and both turned themselves in such a way' — 'girarsi' is reflexive 'to turn (oneself)'. 'che l’uno andasse al primo e l’altro al poi' is a result/relative clause introduced by 'che' followed by the imperfect subjunctive 'andasse' to indicate the manner/result: 'that one went to the first and the other to the next.'
segments.paradiso-13-g13: 'e avrà quasi l’ombra de la vera costellazione e de la doppia danza' — 'avrà' is future of 'avere' ('it will have'); 'quasi' = 'almost/as it were'; 'l’ombra de la vera costellazione' = 'the shadow/likeness of the true constellation'; 'de la doppia danza' = 'and of the double dance'. The relative 'che circulava il punto dov’ io era' gives the action of that dance: 'that made the point where I was circulate/turn.'
segments.paradiso-13-g14: 'poi ch’ è tanto di là da nostra usanza' = 'since/for it is so far beyond our custom' — 'poi ch’' is a causal marker 'since', and 'di là da nostra usanza' = 'far beyond our usual way' (idiomatic).
segments.paradiso-13-g15: 'quanto di là dal mover de la Chiana si move il ciel che tutti li altri avanza' is a comparative clause introduced by 'quanto' = 'as much as' / 'to the extent that'. It compares how far this is 'di là' with the distance by which the sky that is moved by the Chiana advances all the other heavens: 'as far beyond as the sky that moves from the motion of the Chiana moves before all the others.' Note the poetic word order and the use of 'avanza' = 'advances/sets ahead'.
segments.paradiso-13-g16: 'Lì' = 'there' sets the place; 'si cantò' is an impersonal/passive reflexive perfect: 'there was sung' or 'they sang there' (literary passive). 'non Bacco' negates the expected pagan subject 'not Bacchus'.
segments.paradiso-13-g17: Elliptical continuation: 'non Peana' = 'not a paean' (i.e. not a hymn to pagan gods). The short phrase continues the negation begun in the previous segment.
segments.paradiso-13-g18: 'ma tre persone in divina natura' — 'ma' = 'but'; 'tre persone' = theological Persons (Trinity); 'in divina natura' qualifies that these three persons are in the one divine nature. Word order is straightforward noun phrase with prepositional modifier.
segments.paradiso-13-g19: 'e in una persona essa e l’umana' = 'and in one person both it and the human' — 'essa' refers back to the divine nature (the divine) and 'l’umana' = 'the human'; Dante expresses the Christian doctrine that divine and human coexist in one Person (Christ). The construction 'essa e l’umana' is coordinate: 'both the divine and the human.'
segments.paradiso-13-g20: Compié is the passato remoto (third person singular) of compiere, ‘he/it completed’ and appears first (verb‑initial inversion). ’l is the contracted il (the). Cantare is used as a substantive (the act of singing/the song) rather than an active infinitive here. e ’l volger sua misura links a second nominal phrase: volger is a noun formed from the infinitive (the ‘turning’), sua is the possessive referring back to the song/light, and misura is the object of volger (‘measure’ or musical/metrical measure).
segments.paradiso-13-g21: e is the simple conjunction ‘and’. Attesersi is a reflexive past form (si attesero → attesersi in enclitic poetic spelling) meaning ‘they turned themselves / directed themselves / awaited’; the reflexive pronoun is attached. a noi marks the direction toward us (noi as object ‘us’). Quei santi lumi is the delayed subject (demonstrative + adjective + noun) ‘those holy lights’ placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-13-g22: Felicitando is a present participle (‘rejoicing/congratulating’), modifying the subject (the lights) and describing their manner. Sé is the reflexive pronoun referring back to the lights (‘themselves’). Di cura in cura is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ‘from care to care’ or ‘from one charge/concern to another’ (di indicates source/relation and the repetition in cura…cura shows movement from one state/office to the next).
segments.paradiso-13-g23: Ruppe is passato remoto, third person singular of rompere (‘broke’ or ‘interrupted’), placed at the start for dramatic effect. Il silenzio is the direct object. Ne’ concordi numi is a locative phrase ‘among the harmonious numina/deities’ (ne’ = in + i). Poscia is an adverb (‘afterwards/then’) that here precedes the subject la luce, and the relative phrase in che mirabil vita del poverel di Dio narrata modifies luce: in che = ‘in which,’ mirabil vita = ‘wondrous life,’ narrata is a past participle agreeing with vita (‘recounted’).
segments.paradiso-13-g24: e is the coordinating conjunction ‘and’. Disse is passato remoto, third person singular of dire: ‘(it/he) said’. It introduces direct speech.
segments.paradiso-13-g25: Quando is a temporal conjunction ‘when’. L’una paglia = l’ + una + paglia, ‘one straw’ (article + numeral used for emphasis). È trita uses essere (present indicative) + past participle trita used adjectivally: ‘is crushed/ground’ (trita describing the straw’s condition).
segments.paradiso-13-g26: Another temporal clause introduced by quando. La sua semenza is the subject phrase ‘its seed/offspring’ (sua refers back to the straw). È già riposta: present tense of essere + past participle riposta used adjectivally/adverbially ‘is already laid away/put up’; già = ‘already’.
segments.paradiso-13-g27: a + infinitive (a batter) expresses purpose or result here: ‘to strike/beat.’ L’altra is the feminine singular object ‘the other (straw)’. Dolce amor is the subject phrase ‘sweet Love’ (Love personified). M’invita is invita (present indicative, third person singular) with enclitic pronoun m’ = mi (‘me’) attached: ‘(sweet Love) invites/urges me.’
segments.paradiso-13-g28: Main clause beginning with Tu credi che introduces a reported clause (credi che ...). Nel petto onde la costa si trasse is a relative phrase: onde = 'from which' and la costa = 'the rib'; si trasse is a literary/passive-reflexive past form of trarre ('was drawn/taken out'). Per formar expresses purpose ('in order to form') and il cui introduces a relative clause ('whose palate...') that belongs to bella guancia.
segments.paradiso-13-g29: A short connective phrase: e in quel che = 'and in that which...' — que/che introduces the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-13-g30: Forato is a past participle meaning 'pierced' or 'bored through'; da la lancia marks the agent ('by the lance'). The whole phrase modifies the antecedent just mentioned.
segments.paradiso-13-g31: Prima e poscia = 'both before and afterwards' (an emphatic pair). Sodisfece is the literary past tense (passato remoto) of sodisfare ('satisfied' or 'fulfilled') describing how that pierced part acted.
segments.paradiso-13-g32: Che here introduces a result clause: 'so that' (tanto ... che). D’ogne colpa = 'of every fault/sin'; vince is present indicative 'overcomes' and la bilancia is the metaphorical scales (i.e. weighing/justice).
segments.paradiso-13-g33: Quantunque introduces a concessive clause 'although'. A la natura umana lece aver di lume is an archaic construction: lece (from lèce) = 'it is permitted/allowed' and aver di lume = 'to possess/receive light' (spiritual illumination) to the extent human nature may have it.
segments.paradiso-13-g34: Tutto fosse infuso is a subordinate verb phrase in the past subjunctive (fosse) after the perspective set by credi che — here rendered in sense 'all was poured/infused'. Da quel valor = 'by that power/valor' and che l’uno e l’altro fece is a relative clause specifying the power that 'made both the one and the other' (i.e. both parts mentioned).
segments.paradiso-13-g35: E però = 'and therefore'; miri a ciò (miri is a literary subjunctive form here used like an exhortation) = 'turn your attention to / regard what'. Io dissi suso = 'I said above' (suso = 'above').
segments.paradiso-13-g36: Quando narrai = 'when I narrated / when I told (earlier)'; che non ebbe ’l secondo lo ben = 'that the second did not have the good' (ebbe = passato remoto of avere). Che ne la quinta luce è chiuso is a relative clause: 'which is enclosed in the fifth light' (è chiuso = 'is closed/enclosed').
segments.paradiso-13-g37: Or = 'now'; apri is the second-person singular imperative 'open' (addressing the listener). Li occhi = 'the eyes' and a quel ch’io ti rispondo = 'to that which I answer you' (a + relative clause headed by ch’io ... rispondo). Rispondo is present tense 'I answer'.
segments.paradiso-13-g38: Vedräi is a literary future form meaning 'you will see' (or 'you will perceive'); il tuo credere e ’l mio dire are nounified infinitives functioning as objects ('your believing and my saying'). Nel vero farsi = 'become true/in truth' (farsi = 'to become' or 'be made'); come centro in tondo is a simile meaning 'as a center in the round/circle'.
segments.paradiso-13-g39: Ciò che ... non è se non = 'that which ... is nothing but'; the pair 'ciò che non more e ciò che può morire' lists the immortal and the mortal as both being merely splendor (splendor di quella idea). Partorisce is present 'gives birth/ brings forth' and relates to the Idea that generates.
segments.paradiso-13-g40: Amando is a gerund ('loving' / 'in loving') that modifies partorisce — 'which begets ... by loving' — indicating manner or motive.
segments.paradiso-13-g41: Il nostro Sire = 'our Lord' or 'our Sovereign' (Sire referring to God); this closes the clause describing who is begotten by the Idea.
segments.paradiso-13-g42: Ché = 'for' / 'because'; quella viva luce = 'that living light'. The phrase che sì mea dal suo lucente is archaic: here mea is an older/poetic verbal form meaning something like 'draws/takes' (so that the living light receives or takes from its shining source); dal suo lucente = 'from its luminous one/source'.
segments.paradiso-13-g43: Che non si disuna da lui né da l’amor = 'which is not separated from Him nor from the Love' (disuna = 'disunite/separate'); ch’a lor s’intrea is a terse poetic phrase meaning 'that to them is joined/that unites to them' — s’intrea is an archaic reflexive/contracted form describing the union of Love to the two.
segments.paradiso-13-g44: Per sua bontate = 'by his goodness'; il suo raggiare aduna = 'gathers together his radiating' (raggiare = to radiate; aduna = 'gathers/unites'). The subject is God's goodness causing the unity of rays.
segments.paradiso-13-g45: Quasi specchiato = 'as if mirrored' (quasi = 'as if'; specchiato = 'reflected/mirrored'), a participial phrase qualifying how the rays are gathered.
segments.paradiso-13-g46: In nove sussistenze = 'into nine subsistences' (sussistenze here used philosophically for distinct modes/ways of subsisting); in + numeral marks the division into nine.
segments.paradiso-13-g47: Eternalmente rimanendosi una = 'eternally remaining one' — rimanendosi is a reflexive gerund/participle of rimanere ('remaining') and una states that despite the nine subsistences the light remains one in essence.
segments.paradiso-13-g48: Quindi = 'from there/thereupon'; discende a l’ultime potenze = 'it descends to the lowest/ultimate powers'. Giù d’atto in atto is an idiomatic sequence 'down, from act to act' indicating gradual descent/emanation.
segments.paradiso-13-g49: Tanto divenendo = 'becoming so much/so greatly' (tanto ... divenendo), where divenendo is the gerund of divenire ('becoming'). It intensifies the following result.
segments.paradiso-13-g50: Che più non fa che = 'that it no longer does more than' (che introduces the result clause); brevi contingenze = 'brief contingencies' (contingenze = contingent beings or accidental phenomena).
segments.paradiso-13-g51: E queste contingenze essere intendo = 'and by these contingencies I mean' — intendo (from intendere) here = 'I intend/mean' (first person present). Le cose generate = 'the generated/created things' explains what he means by 'contingenze'.
segments.paradiso-13-g52: Che produce con seme e sanza seme il ciel movendo = relative clause 'which [Heaven] produces with seed and without seed, moving'. Con seme e sanza seme describes two modes (with sexual generation and without), and il ciel movendo places the heavenly motion as the producing cause (movendo = gerund 'by moving').
segments.paradiso-13-g100: The clause is an observation with an impersonal construction: 'si parte' is an impersonal/reflexive use meaning 'one sets out' or 'people set out.' 'da riva' is a simple prepositional phrase 'from the shore.' 'più che ’ndarno' is a comparative/adverbial phrase meaning 'more often than in vain' or 'far more than uselessly' where 'andarno' is an archaic form meaning 'in vain' or 'uselessly.' Word order is inverted for emphasis (adverbial phrase fronted).
segments.paradiso-13-g101: 'perché' introduces the reason. 'non torna' is present indicative 'does not return.' 'tal qual' is a fixed expression meaning 'just as' or 'in the same way that.' The word written as 'e’' here is an elided form of 'egli' (he) and functions as the subject, followed by 'si move' (reflexive construction) meaning 'he moves/sets off' — so the whole phrase means 'because he does not come back just as he moved off.'
segments.paradiso-13-g102: 'chi' is the relative/indefinite 'who' (= 'he who' or 'one who'). 'pesca' here means 'fishes' (or 'is engaged in fishing' metaphorically); the lexical entry provided is misleading but the sense is active verb for 'to fish.' 'per lo vero' means 'for/in search of the truth' ('per' marks purpose/aim and 'lo vero' = 'the truth'). 'e non ha l’arte' is 'and lacks the craft/skill' — 'ha' is present indicative 'has' and 'arte' means 'skill, craft, technique' required for successful fishing (literal or figurative).
segments.paradiso-13-g103: 'E di ciò' = 'and of this'; 'sono' is the copula 'are.' 'al mondo' (literally 'to/at the world') means 'in the world' — the phrase 'sono al mondo aperte prove' = 'there are manifest/clear proofs in the world.' The proper name 'Parmenide' follows in apposition as an example.
segments.paradiso-13-g104: A simple list of names and the plural 'molti' ('many'). The conjunction 'e' links the names; 'molti' stands for 'many others.'
segments.paradiso-13-g105: 'li quali' is a relative pronoun ('who' / 'which') referring back to the named philosophers; 'andaro' is an older past form meaning 'they went' (preterite). 'e non sapëan dove' uses the imperfect 'sapëan' = 'they did not know' (ongoing/unspecified knowledge) with 'dove' = 'where.'
segments.paradiso-13-g106: 'sì fé' = 'so did' or 'thus did' (literally 'so made/did'), introducing more examples: Sabellio and Arrio. 'quelli stolti' = 'those fools'; the relative clause 'che furon come spade a le Scritture' = 'who were like swords against the Scriptures' (i.e., destructive toward Scripture). 'in render torti li diritti volti' is an idiomatic infinitive phrase introduced by 'in' meaning 'in making/with the result of making' — 'render torti' = 'to render/cause to be twisted' and 'li diritti volti' = 'the straight/straightforward faces/meanings' (i.e., straightforward texts or plain meanings). Note the archaic past 'furon' = 'furono'.
segments.paradiso-13-g107: 'Non sien' is a negative jussive/hortative phrase: the present subjunctive 'sien' (from essere) is used in a warning or admonition ('let not the people be'). 'le genti' is the subject 'the people.' This construction means 'people should not be/let people not be...'.
segments.paradiso-13-g108: 'ancor' is a parenthetical adverb meaning 'still' or 'yet' (here reinforcing 'not yet'). It stands alone as an interjection-like modifier.
segments.paradiso-13-g109: 'troppo sicure a giudicar' is an adjectival phrase: 'troppo' = 'too,' 'sicure' = 'sure/confident,' and the infinitive 'giudicar' with preposition 'a' expresses the act they are overconfident about — 'too certain in judging.' Note older syntax uses 'a' + infinitive where modern Italian may use other forms.
segments.paradiso-13-g110: 'sì come' introduces a simile ('just as'); 'quei che' = 'those who' or 'someone who'; 'stima' here means 'estimates/assesses' (not honorific 'esteem'); 'le biade in campo' = 'the grain in the field'; 'pria che sien mature' uses 'pria che' + subjunctive 'sien' to mean 'before they are ripe' — subjunctive marks the non-real/anticipated condition.
segments.paradiso-13-g111: 'ch’ i’ ho veduto' = 'that I have seen' (the clitic 'i'' here signals 'io' or is an archaic elision tied to 'che io'); 'ho veduto' is present perfect. 'tutto 'l verno prima' = 'throughout the whole winter beforehand'; 'lo prun mostrarsi' uses the pronominal verb 'mostrarsi' = 'to show itself/appear' and 'rigido e feroce' are predicates describing how it appeared.
segments.paradiso-13-g112: 'poscia' = 'afterwards' or 'later'; 'portar' (infinitive) here denotes the subsequent event — 'then to bear/bring the rose' — 'la rosa in su la cima' = 'the rose up on the summit/top.' The infinitive stands in narrative sequence to the previous perfect.
segments.paradiso-13-g113: This is a perception construction: 'e legno vidi già ... correr lo mar' = 'and I already saw a ship run the sea.' 'vidi' is the perfect of vedere and is followed by an object ('legno') plus an infinitive ('correr') to indicate witnessing an action. 'legno' here commonly means 'ship' (a metonymy in medieval usage). 'dritto e veloce' describe the ship.
segments.paradiso-13-g114: 'perire al fine' = 'to perish in the end' (infinitive expressing the eventual fate). 'a l'intrar de la foce' = 'upon entering the mouth (of the river / the estuary)' — 'de' is an older form of 'di.' The whole final infinitive phrase gives the tragic outcome of the previously seen motion.
segments.paradiso-13-g115: 'Non creda' is a negative jussive/subjunctive in the 3rd person: 'let X not believe' or 'X should not believe.' It addresses 'donna Berta e ser Martino' by name (titles 'donna' and 'ser'), telling them not to hold the following opinion.
segments.paradiso-13-g116: 'per vedere un furare' explains the occasion: 'for/just because they see a theft.' 'per' marks cause or reason here and is followed by the infinitive 'vedere' + the noun phrase 'un furare' ('a stealing' / 'a theft').
segments.paradiso-13-g117: Elliptical infinitive phrase: 'altro offerere' means 'to offer/suppose something else' or 'to infer something different' — 'altro' = 'something else' and 'offerere' = 'to present/offer' (used here in the sense 'to deduce / to put forward').
segments.paradiso-13-g118: 'vederli' = 'to see them' (infinitive + clitic); 'dentro al consiglio divino' = 'within the divine counsel' or 'inside God's counsel/plan.' The phrase warns against imagining that seeing a sinful act means the actors are thereby placed inside divine providence or plan.
segments.paradiso-13-g119: 'ché' introduces the reason/explanation ('for'/'because'). 'quel' is an anaphoric demonstrative pronoun ('that/one thing'), 'può' is present indicative/modal 'may/can,' and 'surgere' (infinitive) = 'to arise' or 'to come up.' The clause gives one pole of the contrast: 'one thing may arise.'
segments.paradiso-13-g120: Parallel to the previous clause: 'e quel può cadere' = 'and that may fall.' The coordination ('e') links two possible outcomes — the point is that appearances can be misleading because different things may come about or fail.

sentences.paradiso-13-s17: I did not speak so as to prevent you from clearly seeing that he was a king who asked for wisdom so that he might be a sufficient king; not in order to know the number in which the movers from here upward are, or whether necessity together with contingency ever made (it) necessary; not to assert that giving the first motion is to be, or whether from a semicircle one can make a triangle so that it would not have a right angle.
sentences.paradiso-13-s18: Therefore, if you mark what I said and this, that unequal sight is royal prudence in which the shaft of my intention strikes; and if you turn your clear eyes to the “risen,” you will see that only respect is given to kings — they are many, and good ones are rare.
sentences.paradiso-13-s19: With this distinction take my saying; and thus it can stand with what you believe about the first Father and our Beloved.
sentences.paradiso-13-s20: And let this always be like lead at your feet, to make you move slowly like a weary man, and as to the yes and the no that you do not see; for he is among the fools of low estate who, without distinction, affirms and denies in one case just as in the other; because he, on the contrary, more often bends prevailing opinion to the false side, and then passion binds the intellect.
sentences.paradiso-13-s9: The wax of these people, and him who molds it, are not of one kind;
sentences.paradiso-13-s10: Whence it happens that one and the same piece of wood, according to species, bears better and worse fruit; and you are born with different natures (ingegno).
sentences.paradiso-13-s11: If the wax were worked to the point and if heaven were in its supreme virtue, the light of the seal would seem altogether; but nature always gives it diminished, similarly acting on the artist whose hand trembles at the practice of art.
sentences.paradiso-13-s12: Therefore, if warm love disposes and marks the clear sight of the first virtue, all perfection is acquired there.
sentences.paradiso-13-s13: Thus the earth was already made worthy of the full perfection of every living creature; thus the Virgin was made pregnant; therefore I commend your opinion that human nature never was nor will be as it was in those two persons.
sentences.paradiso-13-s14: Now, if I were not to go on any further, 'Then — how was this man without equal?'
sentences.paradiso-13-s16: But so that what does not appear may appear clearly, consider who he was and the cause that moved him to ask when he was told 'Ask.'
sentences.paradiso-13-s1: Imagine — whoever wishes to understand well what I have just seen, and keep the image while I speak, like a fixed cliff — fifteen stars which, on different shores, enliven the sky with such calm that they overpower every structure of the air; imagine that chariot whose breast is enough for our heaven both night and day, so that at the turning of time it does not fail; imagine the mouth of that horn which begins at the point of the shaft around which the first wheel goes, having made of itself two signs in the sky, such as Minos’ daughter made when she felt the chill of death; and one had its rays in the other, and both turned in such a way that one went to the first and the other to the next; and it will have, as it were, the shadow of the true constellation and of the double dance that set in motion the point where I was — for it is as far beyond our custom as the sky that moves beyond the motion of the Chiana moves all the others forward.
sentences.paradiso-13-s2: There they sang not Bacchus, not a paean, but three Persons in the divine Nature, and in one Person both the divine and the human.
sentences.paradiso-13-s3: He finished the singing and the turning of its measure; and those holy lights turned toward us, congratulating themselves from one care to another.
sentences.paradiso-13-s4: Then the light in which the marvelous life of God’s poor man was recounted broke the silence among the harmonious powers, and said: “When one straw is crushed, when its seed is already put away, sweet Love bids me beat the other.”
sentences.paradiso-13-s5: Do you believe that in the breast from which the rib was drawn to form the fair cheek, whose palate is prized by the whole world, and in that part which, pierced by the lance, both before and afterwards so satisfied (it), that it outweighs every guilt on the scales — insofar as human nature is permitted to have light — all was poured forth by that power which made both the one and the other; and therefore turn your attention to what I said above, when I told you that the second did not possess the good that is enclosed in the fifth light?
sentences.paradiso-13-s6: Now open your eyes to what I answer you, and you will see your believing and my saying made true in reality, like a center in a circle.
sentences.paradiso-13-s7: That which does not die and that which can die are nothing but the radiance of that Idea which, in loving, brings forth our Lord; for that living light, which so takes from its shining Source and is not separated from Him nor from the Love that is joined to them, by His goodness gathers its radiance together, as if mirrored, into nine subsistences, eternally remaining one.
sentences.paradiso-13-s8: From there it descends to the lowest powers, down from act to act, becoming so much that it does no more than produce brief contingencies; and by these contingencies I mean the generated things that Heaven produces, moving with seed and without seed.
sentences.paradiso-13-s21: Far more often than not one leaves the shore in vain, because he who fishes for the truth and lacks the craft does not return exactly as he set out.
sentences.paradiso-13-s22: And there are open proofs of this in the world: Parmenides, Melissus and Brissus and many others — who went on without knowing where; so did Sabellius and Arius and those fools who were like swords against the Scriptures in making the straight meanings crooked.
sentences.paradiso-13-s23: Let people not yet be too confident in judging, just as someone who judges the grain in the field before it is ripe; for I have seen, all through the winter, the thorn first show itself stiff and fierce, then later bear the rose at its top; and I have already seen a ship, straight and swift, run the sea along its whole course, only to perish at last upon entering the mouth.
sentences.paradiso-13-s24: Let not Donna Berta and Sir Martin believe, when they see a theft, that this proves anything else or that they see the thieves within divine counsel; for one thing may arise, and another may fall.
